edibility: Toxic — frequently mistaken for chanterelles by novice foragers; never eaten. The Jack-O'-Lantern contains illudin S and related compounds that cause severe gastrointestinal poisoning.
taste: Bitter, unpleasant if ingested (not recommended to taste test). The toxins are present regardless of cooking method.
search: Found late summer through fall on hardwood roots, stumps, and buried wood. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: chanterelles have false gills (forked ridges running down the stem), while Jack-O'-Lanterns have true sharp gills (do not fork down the stem) and grow in dense clusters. A unique identifying feature: Jack-O'-Lantern gills bioluminesce with a faint green glow in complete darkness, visible in the field at night.

toxicity.symptoms: Severe gastrointestinal distress including persistent vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and nausea. Symptoms are caused by illudin S and related illudane compounds.
toxicity.treatmentWindow: Immediate medical attention recommended; treatment is primarily supportive as there is no specific antidote
toxicity.antidote: Supportive care including hydration (oral or IV fluids for severe cases), anti-emetics for vomiting, and electrolyte replacement. Most cases resolve within 24 hours with conservative management.
